title: CCI Snapshot Usability Test
subtitle: Snapshot tab + Opportunity Cards
title: CCI Snapshot Usability Test
subtitle: Snapshot tab + Opportunity Cards
description: We're testing whether the Snapshot tab helps users quickly find where to focus and navigate to opportunity cards. We want to learn: (1) Does Snapshot work at all? Can users orient themselves? (2) What's missing or confusing? (3) Can users understand and compare Supply vs Routine Services cards?

rules: We're testing the product, not you. There are no wrong answers.
Please think out loud — tell me what you're looking at and what you're thinking.
If you get stuck, tell me what you expected to happen.
I may stay quiet so I don't bias you.
setup.noHelpPolicy: Give 10 seconds of silence before prompting. If stuck, ask: "What would you do next if I wasn't here?" Do NOT point to UI elements or explain what things do.

warmup.intro: Ask these BEFORE showing the prototype. We want to understand their current mental model.
warmup.why: These questions establish what 'priority' means to this user and what evidence they need to act. This helps us interpret their reactions to Snapshot.

questions.label: Current process
questions.question: When you're trying to find cost reduction opportunities in a service line — how do you typically start? What are the top 2–3 things you look for?
questions.listenFor: What data do they need? What makes something a priority?

questions.label: Recent example
questions.question: Tell me about the last cost initiative you moved forward. What made you confident it was worth pursuing?
questions.listenFor: What evidence do they need? Who do they involve?

tasks.name: First look — exploration
tasks.subtitle: No clicking yet
tasks.script: You have 2 minutes. Without clicking anything, look at this screen and tell me: What is this page showing you? Where would you focus first, and why? Point to something you would NOT focus on and tell me why.
tasks.success: User can articulate what the page is for. Names a specific focus area with a reason tied to data (e.g., 'Supplies has the most money in New'). Can explain why they'd deprioritize something.
watchFor: Do they understand the page purpose without explanation?
What catches their eye first — left panel, right panel, AI banner, KPIs?
Do they notice the category breakdown (Supplies, Routine, Ancillary)?
Do they understand New / Active / Dismissed without explanation?
What do they expect to happen when they click things?
tasks.learnWhy: This tests basic comprehension before any interaction.

tasks.name: First click — natural entry point
tasks.subtitle: Where do they go first?
tasks.script: Now click on the first thing you'd click to go deeper. Talk me through what you're doing and what you expect to see.
tasks.success: User clicks something purposefully (not randomly). Can explain why they chose that entry point. Describes what they expect before seeing the result.
watchFor: What's their instinctive entry point — left panel category? Right panel card? AI banner link?
Do they use the left panel to filter, or go straight to the right panel?
Does their expectation match what actually happens?
If they're surprised, what did they expect instead?
tasks.learnWhy: This reveals their natural navigation pattern.

tasks.name: Filter to a specific priority
tasks.subtitle: Test filtering mechanics
tasks.script: Your VP asks you to focus only on knee replacement opportunities with the highest savings. Show me how you'd find those.
tasks.success: User applies filters (category and/or procedure). Can explain what changed on the right side. Can undo or adjust filters without getting stuck.
watchFor: Do they find the filter controls easily?
Do they understand that the left side controls the right side?
Can they combine multiple filters (category + procedure)?
Do they get lost or confused about the current filter state?
Do they notice the 'Selected' indicator showing current filters?
tasks.learnWhy: This tests whether filtering is discoverable and predictable.

tasks.name: Discover the sorting tabs
tasks.subtitle: Annual Savings / Volume / Overlap
tasks.script: You want to see which opportunities affect the most providers across the organization. How would you find that?
tasks.success: User discovers the 'Overlap' tab or another sorting option. Can explain what the different tabs show.
watchFor: Do they notice the tabs at all?
Do they understand what each tab does?
If they don't find the tabs, what do they try instead?
Is 'Overlap' a meaningful concept to them, or confusing?
tasks.learnWhy: This tests whether the tabs (Annual Savings / Volume / Savings/Case / Overlap) are discoverable.

tasks.name: Customize the view
tasks.subtitle: View dropdown with checkboxes
tasks.script: Now you only want to see Procedures and Providers — not Opportunities or Cost Drivers. How would you do that?
tasks.success: User finds the View dropdown and uses checkboxes to show/hide columns.
watchFor: Do they find the View dropdown?
Do they understand the checkbox pattern (multi-select)?
Is this feature useful to them, or unnecessary complexity?
If they can't find it, what do they try?
tasks.learnWhy: This tests whether the multi-select dropdown is understandable. Skip if running long.

tasks.name: View All — see the full list
tasks.subtitle: Modal with bar chart and table
tasks.script: You want to see the full list of opportunities, not just the top 3. How would you do that? Once you're there, which opportunity would you investigate first?
tasks.success: User clicks 'View All' and sees the modal. Can switch between bar chart and table view. Can identify which opportunity they'd investigate first.
watchFor: Do they find the 'View All' link?
Do they understand the bar chart view?
Do they notice and use the table toggle?
Which view do they prefer and why?
Can they explain how to get from the list to a specific card?
tasks.learnWhy: This tests the View All modal and whether the two display modes make sense.

tasks.name: Open and interpret a Supply card
tasks.subtitle: Navigate + comprehend
tasks.script: Using this screen, find and open one Supply opportunity card. Then tell me: If you had to explain this opportunity to your VP in one sentence, what would you say?
tasks.success: User navigates from Snapshot to a card without wrong turns. Can describe: who's high cost, what the gap is, and at least one driver (use-driven or price-driven).
watchFor: Can they get from Snapshot to a specific card without help?
Do they understand high-cost vs low-cost provider comparison?
Do they understand the cost drivers (use-driven vs price-driven)?
Do they trust the comparison enough to act on it?
What information do they look at first on the card?
tasks.learnWhy: This tests navigation + card comprehension for Supply opportunities.

tasks.name: Interpret a Routine Services card
tasks.subtitle: First draft — exploratory
tasks.script: Now look at this card. It's a different type of opportunity — Routine Services instead of Supplies. Tell me what it's showing you. What do the numbers mean?
tasks.success: User can describe the LOS gap and the dollar translation in their own words. Notes what makes sense and what doesn't.
watchFor: Do they understand the 'length of stay → dollars' story?
Do they understand the benchmark (CMS GMLOS)?
Does the LOS breakdown by department make sense?
What's confusing or missing?
Do they trust the dollar estimate? Would they act on it?
Who do they think should own this type of opportunity?
tasks.learnWhy: This is exploratory — we're testing a first draft. We want impressions, not pass/fail.

note.title: Note on Routine Services
note.content: This card is a first draft. We're not testing whether it 'works' — we're testing whether the concept, structure, and information make sense. Probe with: 'What would you need to see to trust this number?' / 'Who would you bring this to?' / 'What's missing?'

tasks.name: Decide and compare across card types
tasks.subtitle: Prioritization + next steps
tasks.script: You're looking at both a supply card and a routine services card. Which would you pursue first, and why? What would you do next outside the app?
tasks.success: User can compare across card types using dollars. Names a specific next step (meeting, committee, conversation) and who they'd involve.
watchFor: Can they compare supply vs routine using a common metric (dollars)?
What criteria do they use to prioritize?
Do they understand the different actions for each card type?
Who would they involve for each type?
Do they feel confident enough to act, or do they need more information?
tasks.learnWhy: This tests whether users can prioritize across different opportunity types.

wrapup.intro: Ask these after all tasks are complete.

questions.label: Overall impression
questions.question: Now that you've seen the whole flow — what worked well? What was frustrating or confusing?

questions.label: Missing information
questions.question: Was there anything you expected to see that wasn't there?

questions.label: Trust
questions.question: Would you trust this tool to guide a conversation with a physician or your VP? Why or why not?

questions.label: Preference
questions.question: On the main Snapshot screen — did you prefer seeing opportunities by category, or would you rather see one unified list ranked by savings?

observerNotes.meaning: Banner isn't prominent enough, or users don't trust AI summaries
Tabs aren't discoverable, or default view is sufficient

observerNotes.behavior: Clicks left panel but expects different result
observerNotes.meaning: Mental model mismatch — they don't understand left filters right
Link isn't prominent enough, or they don't need more than top 3
Multi-select pattern is unexpected — consider simplifying

observerNotes.behavior: Can explain Supply card but not Routine Services
observerNotes.meaning: LOS → dollars translation needs work, or structure is unclear

observerNotes.behavior: Says 'I'd need to talk to someone' for Routine Services
observerNotes.meaning: Card doesn't give enough info to act — probe for what's missing

observerNotes.behavior: Prefers table over bar chart (or vice versa)
observerNotes.meaning: Consider defaulting to preferred view, or removing the other
